coredata.dc:title: Si-assisted N, P Co-doped room temperature phosphorescent carbonized polymer Dots: Information Encryption, graphic Anti-counterfeiting and biological imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yang
Chen, Weihua
Lu, Lehui
Yang, Bai
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have been widely noticed due to their superior optical properties in the past decade. Herein, we report the Si-assisted N, P co-doped RTP carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), prepared by one-step microwave assisted heating of 3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyldimethoxymethylsilane and phosphoric acid aqueous solution. The obtained CPDs exhibit bright RTP with an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield 34.17%, absolute phosphorescence quantum yield 11.42% and long lifetime of 1.42 s, which lasts for about 16 s to the naked eyes. We apply the obtained CPDs into information encryption and Polyethyleneterephthalate (PET) film graphic anti-counterfeiting with facile way. Additionally, we make more efforts to realize aqueous RTP CPDs composite. The obtained aqueous CPDs composite exhibits bright aqueous RTP with absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of 12.61%, absolute phosphorescence quantum yield (QY) of 5.08% and RTP lifetime of 1.46 s, which lasts for about 10 s to the naked eyes. The RTP CPDs composite is nontoxic and biocompatible. These brilliant characteristics allow CPDs composite to be applicable in biological imaging. Thisstudy affords a new RTP material which can be easily prepared and used for information encryption, graphic anti-counterfeiting and biological imaging
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Si-assisted N
P co-doped
Information encryption
Anti-counterfeiting
Biological imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The long afterglow mechanism of CPDs-6
The information encryption and graphic anti-counterfeiting applications of CPDs-6
Preparation and characterization of CPDs-6 composite
The biological imaging of CPDs-6 composite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image (inset: HRTEM), b) size distribution histogram, c) FT-IR, d) XPS and e-i) HRXPS spectra and fitting results (e: O 1 s, f: N 1 s, g: C 1 s, h: P 2p and i: Si 2p) of CPDs-6.

a) The normalized UV–Vis absorbance spectra (black line), PL emission spectra (blue line) and PL excitation spectra (red line) of CPDs-6 in H2O. b) The normalized UV–Vis absorbance spectra (black line), PL emission spectra (blue line), PL excitation spectra (red line) and phosphorescence emission spectra (pink line) of CPDs-6 solid powder. c) Phosphorescence decay curves of CPDs-6 solid powder at ambient conditions (excitation wavelength was set at 365 nm). d) Temperature-dependent PL emission spectra of CPDs-6 solid powder. e) Time-resolved phosphorescence emission spectra of CPDs-6 solid powder under 365 nm excitation (1–9 s). f) Proposed Absorption, FL and phosphorescence emission processes of CPDs-6 solid powder.

a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) of CPDs-6@SiO2. b) Phosphorescence emission spectrum of CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution. c) UV–Vis absorbance spectrum, photoluminescence excitation spectrum of CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution. d) Afterglow decay curve and lifetime of CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution monitored at 520 nm with 365 nm excitation. e) FTIR spectra of CPDs-6, CPDs-6@SiO2 and SiO2. f) XPS spectra of CPDs-6 and CPDs-6@SiO2.

a) Cytotoxicity of CPDs-6@SiO2 (n = 6, mean ± SD). b) Quantification of phosphorescence intensity of CPDs-6@SiO2 as a function of the delay time after UV lamp off using software ImageJ (n = 3, mean ± SD). c) Bright-field, fluorescence, and phosphorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with CPDs-6@SiO2 for 2 h. Scale bar: 10 μm.

a) Confocal images of HeLa cells incubated with CPDs-6@SiO2 and Lyso-Tracker Red (a red fluorescence dye for lysosome). Scale bar: 10 μm. b) Confocal images of HeLa cells incubated with CPDs-6@SiO2 and DiI (a red fluorescence dye for cell membrance). Scale bar: 10 μm. c) The co-localization analyses of CPDs-6@SiO2 and Lyso-Tracker Red that marked in (a). d) The co-localization analyses of CPDs-6@SiO2 and DiI that marked in (b). e) The fluorescence and phosphorescence intensities of CPDs-6@SiO2 in different pH.

a) Phosphorescence images of CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution with different concentration. b) Quantification of phosphorescence intensity of CPDs-6@SiO2 with different concentration using software ImageJ (n = 3, mean ± SD). c) Phosphorescence and fluorescence imaging of a mouse with the subcutaneous inclusions of CPDs-6@SiO2. The circles indicate the location of nanoparticle inclusions. d) Quantification of phosphorescence and fluorescence intensity of the subcutaneous inclusion of CPDs-6@SiO2 and of the background (BG) tissue without containing CPDs-6@SiO2 using software ImageJ (n = 3, mean ± SD). e) The body weight changes (n = 8, mean ± SD), f) liver biochemical assay (n = 3, mean ± SD), g) blood hematology analysis (n = 3, mean ± SD). Student's test was employed for statistical analyses, and the asterisks corresponded to different P values: *** p > 0.2, **p > 0.1, *p > 0.05.

head.ce:title: Si-assisted N, P Co-doped room temperature phosphorescent carbonized polymer Dots: Information Encryption, graphic Anti-counterfeiting and biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Changchun 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Changchun 130022, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yang Liu and Weihua Chen are co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have been widely noticed due to their superior optical properties in the past decade. Herein, we report the Si-assisted N, P co-doped RTP carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), prepared by one-step microwave assisted heating of 3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyldimethoxymethylsilane and phosphoric acid aqueous solution. The obtained CPDs exhibit bright RTP with an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield 34.17%, absolute phosphorescence quantum yield 11.42% and long lifetime of 1.42 s, which lasts for about 16 s to the naked eyes. We apply the obtained CPDs into information encryption and Polyethyleneterephthalate (PET) film graphic anti-counterfeiting with facile way. Additionally, we make more efforts to realize aqueous RTP CPDs composite. The obtained aqueous CPDs composite exhibits bright aqueous RTP with absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of 12.61%, absolute phosphorescence quantum yield (QY) of 5.08% and RTP lifetime of 1.46 s, which lasts for about 10 s to the naked eyes. The RTP CPDs composite is nontoxic and biocompatible. These brilliant characteristics allow CPDs composite to be applicable in biological imaging. Thisstudy affords a new RTP material which can be easily prepared and used for information encryption, graphic anti-counterfeiting and biological imaging

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with long afterglow have been widely concerned in the fields of optoelectronic devices, information encryption, anti-counterfeiting and biological imaging [1–9]. So far, most reported RTP materials have been mainly developed by transition metal-containing or rare-earth ions inorganic materials, metal-containing organic complexes and specific pure organic compounds [1,2,8,10–20]. These materials have obvious shortcomings. Metal-containing materials need harsh preparation conditions or expensive rare metals. Organic materials have relatively short afterglow lifetime, low stability, high cost, highly toxicity, complex preparation process, etc., which extensively restrict their practical applications. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new kind of photonic nanomaterial, have been widely concerned because of their brilliant optical properties, abundant precursors source, easy preparation, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and superior stability. Therefore, CDs have been widely used in many fields such as optoelectronic, information encryption, sensors, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis and biological imaging [21–32]. Up to now, most kinds of CDs have been developed in the field of fluorescence rather than phosphorescence due to the spin-forbidden transition feature of triplet-excited states and the non-radiative deactivation. Even so, RTP CDs have still been developed in recent years. Basically, RTP CDs are implemented in two forms. One of them requires the assistance of additional matrix, such as Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polyurethane (PU), KAl(SO4)2·xH2O, silica gel, urea/biuret and boric acid which can fix CDs and stabilize excited triplet states [33–38]. The other is matrix-free, such as polymer carbon dots, Ultra-long room temperature phosphorescence (URTP) carbon dots (CDs), single-component white-light-emissive carbonized polymer dots (SW-CPDs) and so on, which were related to crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect [3,6,7,39–41].

According to the above thinking, we designed and synthesized RTP CDs without additional matrix, named as CPDs-6. The CPDs-6 was prepared by a fast one-step microwave-assisted heating 3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyldimethoxymethylsilane and phosphoric acid aqueous solution (Fig. S1(a)). It took only 6 min to finish the microwave reaction and then followed by dialysis purification for 24 h and freeze-drying for 48 h (see experimental section for details). The CPDs-6 exhibit bright yellow-green RTP with an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield 34.17%, absolute phosphorescence quantum yield 11.42% and a lifetime of 1.42 s, which lasts for about 16 s to the naked eyes. Due to its long afterglow characteristic, the CPDs-6 was successfully applied in information encryption and printed film graphic anti-counterfeiting. Considering the brilliant character of CPDs-6, we made more effort to make it to be CPDs composite which can produce RTP in aqueous solution.

3-(2-Aminoethylamino) propyldimethoxymethylsilane was purchased from TCI. N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine was purchased from J&K ®. Phosphoric acid was purchased from MIDHEAVEN FINE. Ethane diamine was purchased from Alfa Aesar. 3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyltriethoxysilane was purchased from Aladdin. Tetraethyl silicate was purchased from BeiJing Chemical Works. All reagents were analytical reagents. Deionized water was used in the experiments. All of the chemicals were used without further purification.

TEM images were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100F. The FT-IR spectrum was acquired by a VERTEX 80 V FT-IR spectrophotometer (Brucker). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a mono X-ray source with Al Ka excitation (1486.6 eV). PL spectrum was performed on a RF-6000 spectrophotometer. Time-resolved PL spectra, temperature-dependent transient decays, temperature-dependent transient PL spectra and absolute PL QY were acquired on an FLS 920 using an integrating sphere (Edinburgh). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of solids and solution were performed with a U-4100 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Hitachi) and a 3100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu), respectively. RTP lifetime was measured by FLS 980 (Edinburgh) under 365 nm. The absolute phosphorescence QYs were obtained using an Edinburgh FLS980 FL spectrophotometer.

3-(2-Aminoethyl -amino) propyldimethoxymethylsilane (AEAPMS) (1.4 ml) was dissolved in deionized water (4 ml),then stirred the aqueous solution and dropped with 1 ml phosphoric acid. Transfer the aqueous solution of the formed transparent mixture to a 250 ml beaker and heat in a household oven (350 W) for 6 min. After reaction, the reactors were naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained gelatinous solid was dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water for ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The coarse aqueous solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm/min) for 10 min and the large particles or agglomerated particles were filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. The collected solution was dialyzed (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h (water was changed every 6 h). Finally, CPDs-6 was obtained as a pale yellow powder by freeze-drying 48 h. (Other CPDs (such as CPDs-1, CPDs-2, etc.)use the same synthesis method, but the reaction time is different).

N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (1.42 ml) was dissolved in deionized water (4 ml), then stirred the aqueous solution and dropped with 1 ml phosphoric acid. Transfer the solution of the formed transparent mixture to a 250 ml beaker and heat in a household oven (350 W) for 6 min. After reaction, the reactors were naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained gelatinous solid was dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water for ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The coarse aqueous solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm/min) for 10 min and the large particles or agglomerated particles were filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. The collected solution was dialyzed (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h (water was changed every 6 h). Finally, CPDs-II was obtained as a pale yellow powder by freeze-drying 48 h.

3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyltriethoxysilane (1.84 ml) was dissolved in deionized water (4 ml), then stirred the aqueous solution and dropped with 1 ml phosphoric acid. Transfer the solution of the formed transparent mixture to a 250 ml beaker and heat in a household oven (350 W) for 6 min. After reaction, the reactors were naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained gelatinous solid was dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water for ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The coarse aqueous solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm/min) for 10 min and the large particles or agglomerated particles were filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. The collected solution was dialyzed (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h (water was changed every 6 h). Finally, CPDs-III was obtained as a pale yellow powder by freeze-drying 48 h.

Ethane diamine (3 ml) was dissolved in deionized water (15 ml), and then 2 ml of phosphoric acid was added into the aqueous solution drop by drop under agitation. Transfer the solution of the formed transparent mixture to a 250 ml beaker and heat in a domestic oven (350 W) for 3 min. After reaction, the reactors were naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained gelatinous solid was dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water for ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The coarse aqueous solution was centrifuged for 10 min (10000 rpm /min), and the large particles or agglomerated particles were filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then neutralized with potassium carbonate. The collected solution was dialyzed (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h (water was changed every 6 h). Finally, the CPDs-N was obtained as a pale yellow powder by freeze-drying 48 h [42].

Briefly, CPDs-6 (30 mg), deionized water (40 ml) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) (1.5 ml) were added to a 100 ml round-bottom flask stirring at room temperature for a few minutes. And then the combined system was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100 ml) and heated at 100℃ for 10 h in oven. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained transparent solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm/min for 10 min) and filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove large or agglomerated particles. Then it was fully dialyzed in dialysis bag (3500 D) against deionized water for a week to remove small particles. The aqueous CPDs@SiO2 was got by transfer it to glass vial from dialysis bag. (The solid-state CPDs@SiO2 material was obtained from aqueous state by freeze-drying, but it could not dissolved in water any more)

The cytotoxicity of CPDs-6@SiO2 to cells was determined by MTT assay. HeLa cells were seeded into 96-well plates and cultured for 24 h. Then the medium was replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium containing different concentrations of CPDs-6@SiO2 (50, 100, 200, 400 and 800 μg/ml). After incubation for 24 h, the medium was replaced by MTT solution in DMEM medium (0.5 mg/ml, 100 μL) and incubated for another 4 h. Then the supernatants were carefully removed, and 100 μL DMSO was added into each well. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured on a microtiter plate reader. The untreated HeLa cells served as control. Cell viability was calculated by the following equation:
CellViablility =
A b s . ( 570 , s a m p l e )
A b s . ( 570 , c o n t r o l )
× 100 %

The biocompatibility of CPDs-6@SiO2 in vivo was studied by intravenously injecting CPDs-6@SiO2 (20 mg/kg) into the ICR mice with an average weight of about 20 g. The body weight was recorded once four days for one month. The blood was collected for blood biochemistry assay and hematology analysis and the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) were collected for histology studies at 7 days and at one month after injection. The untreated mice were used as control.

Hela cells were seeded into the 35 mm glass culture dish and cultured at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. Then the medium was replaced with 2 ml of fresh medium containing CPDs-6@SiO2 (360 μg/ml), respectively. After incubation for 2 h, the cells were washed with PBS for three times before bioimaging. For fluorescence imaging, HeLa cells were imaged by a laser scanning confocal microscope (CLSM) with excitation of 405 nm, and the collecting wavelengths were 417–477 nm. The phosphorescence images were captured immediately after the removal of excitation, and the collecting wavelengths were 500–550 nm.

To investigate the inherent cell location of CPDs-6@SiO2, the Hela cells were incubated with CPDs-6@SiO2 (360 μg/ml) for 2 h. Then, the treated cells were co-stained with DiI (cell membrance red fluorescence probe, Beyotime Biotechnology) and Lyso-Tracker Red (lysosome red fluorescence probe, Beyotime Biotechnology), and finally, observed by CLSM.

ROS generation inside cells under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation was detected using DCF-DA Cellular Reactive Species Assay Kit (Beyotime Biotechnology). Hela cells were seeded in 35 mm glass culture dish and incubated for 24 h. The medium was replaced with CPDs-6, CPDs-6@SiO2 (800 μg/ml), respectively. After incubation for 2 h, DCF-DA was added according to the standard protocol provided by the suppliers. After 10 min incubation, cells were washed three times with PBS and irradiated by the 365 nm UV lamp (5–10 W) for 5 min. The UV lamp was put 1 cm above the cells. Then the medium was replaced with PBS and fluorescence images of the treated cells were acquired by using a fluorescence microscope.

Ultra-long phosphorescence images of CPDs-6@SiO2 solution were acquired with the IVIS spectrum imaging system at t = 2 s after light irradiated by 365 nm hand-held UV lamp (5–10 W) for 1 min. The UV lamp was put 1 cm above the tubes to activate these CDs. The IVIS spectrum imaging system was set under the bioluminescence mode with open filter and an imaging acquisition time of 20 s.

Female nude mice received subcutaneous injections of CPDs-6@SiO2 (100 μL, 7.5 mg/ml) into the dorsal areas of mice. After injection, in-situ activation of ultra-long phosphorescence was conducted by irradiation with a 365 nm hand-held UV lamp (5–10 W) for 1 min. The UV lamp was put 1 cm above the injection sites. Then, the images were acquired at t = 2 s after removal of light source using IVIS living imaging system. The signal acquisition was conducted for 20 s under the bioluminescence mode with open filter setting. For comparison, the vivo fluorescence imaging of mice were also carried out (excitation: 385 ± 20 nm; emission: 535 ± 20 nm).
The CPDs aqueous dispersion (50 mg/ml) can be directly used as security inks for anti-counterfeiting.

The blank digital pattern “8888” is painted on the paper with three different CDs. First, we used CPDs-6 marking the character “2021”. Second, the shorter and weaker afterglow CPDs-N was added to increase four marks to make “2021” become “2020”. Eventually, the fluorescence CDs without phosphorescence filled the remaining marks to make “2020” to be “8888”.

The morphology of CPDs-6 was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 (a), (b), CPDs-6 exhibited well dispersed quasi-spherical particles with an average particle size about 4.9 nm and particle size distribution in the range of 4.2–5.6 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (inset of Fig. 1(a)) shows that CPDs-6 has the lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which attributed to the 001 facet of graphite [43].

The surface functional groups and elements compositions of CPDs-6 were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the FTIR spectrum of CPDs-6 has absorption bands at 3426 cm−1 and multiple peaks at 2733–3018 cm−1, respectively distributed in stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (OH), amino (NH) and (CH2-) bonds [7]. The peak located at 1471 cm−1 corresponding to the bending vibration of (CH-) [7]. The absorption peaks at 1261 and 1176 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration of (PO) and (CN), respectively [8]. The absorption peak 1076 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of (CO)/(PO) or asymmetric stretching of (SiOSi)/(SiOC) [7,43]. The absorption peak 900 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of (POP). The characteristic peak (SiC) is located at 790 cm−1. Absorption peaks at 2403 and 521 cm−1 indicate the presence of amines and phosphates [7]. The absorption peak of CPDs-6 at 971 cm−1 may be the (PN) bond formed during condensation [8].

The XPS characterization can further confirm the FT-IR results. XPS results show that CPDs-6 is mainly composed of C, N, O, P and Si elements with binding energy at 284.6, 401.3, 532.4, 133.5 and 101.6 eV, respectively, which are attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, P 2p and Si 2p (Fig. 1(d)). Their element contents were calculated to be 37.45, 8.45, 36.95, 10.73, and 6.42%, respectively. The high resolution spectra of O 1 s indicate the presence of PO at 530.6 eV, PO at 532.4 eV, SiOP at 532.8 eV (Fig. 1(e)) [43]. The high-resolution spectra of N 1 s show the presence of N(C)3 at 400.6 eV and NH at 401.3 eV (Fig. 1(f)) [7]. The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s weredeconvolved into five peaks at 283.9, 284.6, 285.4, 286.1 and 286.6 eV, which are derived from CSi, CC/CC, CN/CN, COSi and CO, respectively (Fig. 1(g)) [7,43]. The P 2p contains two peaks at 133.2 eV and 133.9 eV for OPO and OPN (Fig. 1(h)) [7]. The Si 2p spectra can be deconvolved into two peaks at 101.4 eV and 101.8 eV for CSi and OCSi (Fig. 1(i)). The N, O and P elements inside of CPDs-6 can favor spin–orbit coupling (SOC) and form hydrogen bonds which will confine the movement of luminescent units [42].

The optical properties of CPDs-6 were investigated as shown in Fig. 2 (a)–(f). Fig. 2(a) shows the normalized UV–Vis absorption spectrum, PL emission spectrum and PL excitation spectrum of CPDs-6 aqueous solution. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CPDs-6 aqueous solution shows two typical characteristic absorption peaks at 285 and 346 nm, which may be respectively assigned to the π–π* and n–π* transitions of certain sub-fluorophores (e.g., CC, CN, CN, CO and PO) (Fig. 2(a)) [7]. Furthermore, the PL excitation spectrum and absorption spectrum of CPDs-6 aqueous solution are in good agreement, indicating that the PL emission is originated from the same transitions. Under UV irradiation, CPDs-6 aqueous solution shows blue PL luminescence and no phosphorescence at room temperature. The absolute PL quantum yield (QY) of CPDs-6 aqueous solution was determined to be 12.65% using an integrated sphere at 365 nm excitation.

As shown in Fig. S1b and video S1, CPDs-6 powder can emit bright long-lived yellow-green RTP after ceasing the 365 nm UV lamp. Fig. 2(b) shows the normalized UV–Vis absorption spectrum, PL emission spectrum, excitation spectrum and phosphorescence emission spectrum of CPDs-6 solid powder. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CPDs-6 solid powder shows obvious absorption peaks at around 290 nm and 353 nm which are close to photoluminescence (PL) excitation spectrum of CPDs-6 solid powder and can be assigned to the π–π* transition and n-π* transition [7]. CPDs-6 solid powder displays the similar PL emission spectrum to its aqueous solution while the presence of phosphorescence may be responsible for the shoulder peak at around 515 nm since the afterglow spectrum of CPDs-6 solid powder shows the phosphorescence emission mainly centered at 515 nm.

The absolute PL QY and phosphorescence QY of CPDs-6 solid powder are 34.17% and 11.42%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the afterglow decay curve of CPDs-6 was measured which has been fitted with a tri-exponential function with lifetime of 1.42 s. The temperature dependent transient decay curves of CPDs-6 solid powder were recorded from 78 to 353 K (Fig. S2). Its lifetime decreases with the increase of temperature, which is the typical characteristic of phosphorescent materials. The temperature-dependent PL emission spectra of CPDs-6 solid powder can also prove its phosphorescence character since the phosphorescence peak (515 nm) decrease much lower than fluorescence peak as the temperature goes higher (as shown in Fig. 2(d)). In addition, the time-resolved emission spectra (TRES) of CPDs-6 solid powder under 365 nm excitation were measured (Fig. 2(e) and Fig. S3), revealing the long afterglow properties. The phosphorescence spectra of solid powder CPDs-6 under different excitation wavelengths show excitation-independent character with optimal emission centered at around 515 nm (Fig. S4a). The afterglow decay curves of CPDs-6 solid powder under different excitation wavelengths were measured which have been fitted with a tri-exponential function (Fig. S4b and Table S4). CPDs-6 can exhibit visible RTP after removing UV excitation even in broad daylight which is very competitive in practical applications. The proposed luminescence process schematic of CPDs-6 can be simply shown as Fig. 2(f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The long afterglow mechanism of CPDs-6
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a deeper insight into long afterglow of CPDs-6, several CPDs through controlling different microwave reaction time were synthesized whose corresponding lifetime shown in Fig. S5 and Table S1. The optimal reaction time is 6 min (CPDs-6). For convenience, we selected another two obtained CPDs for comparison to detect the mechanism of the long afterglow and named them to be CPDs-1 and CPDs-2 whose corresponding microwave reaction time are 1 min and 2 min, respectively. Contrasted with CPDs-6, CPDs-1 and CPDs-2 have shorter lifetime whose emission images with the UV on and off at room temperature (Fig. S6). As shown in Figs. S7(a) and S7(b), both CPDs-1 and CPDs-2 are quasi-spherical amorphous particles with good dispersion, and the average particle size is about 3.72 nm and 5.07 nm, respectively, as observed in TEM. No obvious lattice fringes but polymer like structures were observed in CPDs-1 and CPDs-2 (Inset of Figs. S7(a) and S7(b) in the supporting information), indicating that the polymeric structure formed. As shown in Fig. S8 and Fig. 1c, the FTIR spectra of three CPDs are similar. Among them, the absorption peak of CPDs-6 at 971 cm−1 attributed to (PN) bond formed during the condensation process, which can weakly be observed in the FTIR spectra of CPDs-1 and CPDs-2. It could conclude that PN bond may be important for long afterglow of CPDs-6 based on the different structures of CPDs-1, CPDs-2, and CPDs-6. XPS results show that the three CPDs are all mainly composed of C, N, O, P and Si elements (Fig. 1(d) and Fig. S9). To confirm the significance of Si, we prepared another three CPDs (CPDs-N, CPDs-II and CPDs-III) by using other raw materials (ethane diamine and other organosilanes) to replace 3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyldimethoxymethylsilane under the similar or same reaction conditions (synthesis and characterization of the corresponding products being supplied in the supporting information, Figs. S10–S21, Table S2). In the case of CPDs-N (ethane diamine as raw materials), weaker and shorter afterglow can be observed (Fig. S10). For the other two organosilanes (CPDs-II and CPDs-III) case, the similar afterglow to CPDs-6 can be observed (Figs. S14 and S18), positively demonstrating the importance of Si.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The information encryption and graphic anti-counterfeiting applications of CPDs-6
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its wonderful properties, CPDs-6 was dissolved in water and used as security ink for multi information encryption (methods details see experimental section part). As shown in the up part of Fig. S22, when excited by a 365 nm UV lamp, the pattern of the model appeared in blue letters “8888″. And then the yellow-green number “2020” can be captured by the naked eye after ceasing 365 nm UV. Eventually, the designed model exhibited a unique “2021” pattern after 3 s. It is a simple but effective information encryption way. Furthermore, by doping 10% Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) into CPDs-6 security inks (PVA were used for increasing the viscosity of CPDs-6 security ink), graphic anti-counterfeiting ink has been designed. As shown in the down part of Fig. S22, the “butterfly and flower” were printed on a smooth PET film. When the UV excitation on, there will be blue graphic. And when the excitation off, there will be green graphic showing off.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CPDs-6 composite
ce:section.ce:para: Impressively, base on the SiO covalent bonds of CPDs-6, we can easily cover a SiO2 protector. CPDs-6 was covered with silica layer to avoid water and oxygen by reacting with tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) during hydrolysis process in autoclave at 100 °C (Fig. S1(a) and Experimental section). The aqueous CPDs-6@SiO2 composite was obtained after the reaction with RTP property. After removing UV irradiation, the bright yellow-green phosphorescence of CPDs-6@SiO2 solution can be easily observed to the naked eye within about 10 s, as shown in Fig. S1(c) and video S2. The time-resolved emission spectra (TRES) of CPDs-6@SiO2 solution under 365 nm excitation were measured (Fig. S23), revealing the long afterglow properties. It can be seen from TEM (Fig. 3 (a)) that CPDs-6 is surrounded by a spherical silicone shell. Because the outer shell is silicon dioxide, the outside water cannot enter the interior CPDs-6 to destroy RTP. The silica shell will also further limit the vibration and rotation of CPDs-6. The afterglow spectrum of CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution exhibited a peak centered at around 520 nm with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 3(b)), which is a little shift compared with the CPDs-6 powder. The excitation and absorption spectra of the CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution exhibit two consistent peaks (Fig. 3(c)) at around 290 and 350 nm, which may derive from the π → π* and n → π* transitions of certain function groups similar like CPDs-6 powder. They may be responsible for their photoluminescence and phosphorescence emission. The lifetime decay curve of the CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution collected at 520 nm is shown in Fig. 3(d), and its lifetime is 1.46 s. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of the CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution was 12.61% and absolute phosphorescence quantum yield was 5.08%. According to the FTIR results, CPDs-6@SiO2 is just like the combination of CPDs-6 and SiO2 (Fig. 3(e) and Fig. 1(c)). Comparing the XPS spectra of CPDs-6 and CPDs-6@SiO2, there are two obvious differences (Fig. 3(f) and Fig. 1(d)). One is the N peak of CPDs-6@SiO2 is very weak, the other is the P peak of CPDs-6@SiO2 disappears. The above results indicated that the silicon shell covered the CPDs-6. The CPDs-6@SiO2 composite aqueous solution has the potential of biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The biological imaging of CPDs-6 composite
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of CPDs-6@SiO2 was evaluated by MTT assays. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the cell viabilities of HeLa cells was above 88% even with the concentration 800 ug/ml, indicating a very low cytotoxicity of CPDs-6@SiO2. The ultra-long room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and low biological toxicity endowed CPDs-6@SiO2 with great potential in diagnostic applications. To demonstrate this, HeLa cells were incubated with CPDs-6@SiO2 for 2 h and then washed with PBS buffer for three times, their luminescence images were obtained by a laser scanning confocal microscope (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 4c, the strong blue emission signal of fluorescence image under 405 nm laser excitation indicated that CPDs-6@SiO2 could rapidly and efficiently enter and label HeLa cells. In addition, turning off the excitation source, room temperature phosphorescence images were collected at different time intervals in the range of 500–550 nm. The phosphorescence intensity decreased over delay time, and green phosphorescence signal still existed even with the delay time of 600 ms (Fig. 4(b)), demonstrating a glorious capacity of afterglow bioimaging.

Next, to investigate their cellular fate, HeLa cells treated with CPDs-6@SiO2 were co-stained with DiI (a red fluorescence dye for cell membrane) and Lyso-Tracker Red (a red fluorescence dye for lysosome). In Fig. 5 (a), the blue fluorescence signal and green phosphorescence signal of CPDs-6@SiO2 didn't overlap effectively, meaning their properties of fluorescence and phosphorescence might change in cellular environment. To explore this, HeLa cells treated with CPDs-6@SiO2 were stained with Lyso-Tracker Red. Interesting, the section, which was brighter in phosphorescence channel than that in fluorescence channel, was effective co-located with lysosomes (Fig. 5(a) and Fig. 5(c)). With in mind of the acidic environment (pH = 4.5–6) in lysosomes and neutral environment (pH ≈ 7) in other organelles, the pH-dependent optical property was assessed. As shown in Fig. 5(e), the fluorescence intensity showed no obvious variation, while the phosphorescence intensity at pH 6 was around three times as strong as that at pH 7. Therefore, the phosphorescence signal in lysosomes was higher than that in other neutral organelles. Next, colocalization assay of cell membrane and CPDs-6@SiO2 was carried out. Significantly, the fluorescence signal coincided well with cell membrane signal (Fig. 5(b) and 5(d)), indicating that CPDs-6@SiO2 could also image cell membrane, which was resulted from the strong electrostatic interaction between CPDs-6@SiO2 with high positive charges and the cell membrane with negative charges. What's more, CPDs-6@SiO2 didn't produce cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells (Fig. S24). Therefore, CPDs-6@SiO2 could simultaneously probe the cell membrane and lysosomes without damage to cells.

To find out if CPDs-6@SiO2 could be employed in afterglow imaging, the phosphorescence images of its aqueous solutions with different concentrations were collected by an IVIS living imaging system under the bioluminescence mode where the excitation light is off during imaging process. That is, CPDs-6@SiO2 was irradiated by UV light for 1 min to be activated. Then, the phosphorescence images were acquired at t = 2 s after removal of light source with an imaging acquisition time of 20 s. As shown in Fig. 6 (a)–(b), the phosphorescence of the CPDs-6@SiO2 was easily detected and the phosphorescence intensity increased with increase their concentration. CPDs-6@SiO2 was used to evaluate possibility for in vivo afterglow imaging. The CPDs-6@SiO2 was injected in subcutaneous of the dorsal area of mice and illuminated for 1 min. Subsequently, the images were acquired for 20 s under the bioluminescence mode after removing the excitation light for 2 s. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the phosphorescence image of CPDs-6@SiO2 was easily obtained. In contrast, because of the strong endogenous autofluorescence of mice, it was difficult to distinguish the fluorescence signals of CPDs-6@SiO2 from the background tissue. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the in vivo phosphorescence intensity of CPDs-6@SiO2 was 1500-fold higher than background tissue due to the really low background noise, while the fluorescence intensity was 1.05 times that caused the undetectability. These results suggested the successful phosphorescence imaging of CPDs-6@SiO2 in vivo, highlighting the advantage of long-lived phosphorescence in bioimaging by eliminating the autofluorescence interference.

The potential long-term toxicity of CPDs-6@SiO2 in vivo was studied by intravenously injecting CPDs-6@SiO2 (20 mg/kg) into the ICR mice. Firstly, the body weight was recorded once four days for one month. As shown in Fig. 6(e), the body weight of the groups treated with CPDs-6@SiO2 showed no obvious variation compared with the control group, indicating their no noticeable systemic toxicity. In addition, the blood was collected for blood biochemistry assay and hematology analysis, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6(f) and 6(g), the serum and complete blood test parameters of experimental groups had no obvious difference with those of control group, indicating the favorable biocompatibility in vivo. Furthermore, the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were collected for histological examinations using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. There was no obvious tissue damage or inflammatory lesion in all major organs (Fig. S25). These results suggested that CPDs-6@SiO2 has negligible toxicity in vivo for applications in biological image.

In summary, silicon-assisted room temperature long afterglow carbon dots (CPDs-6) were prepared by one-step microwave assisted heating of phosphoric acid and 3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyldimethoxymethylsilane aqueous solution. The obtained CPDs-6 has 1.42 s long-lived RTP lifetime and absolute phosphorescence QY of 11.42% (365 nm excitation), the afterglow of which lasts for about 16 s to the naked eyes. The presence of the doping elements N, P and Si are responsible for the observed long afterglow of the CPDs. The doped N and P atoms provide triplet excitons and form CN/CN/PN bonds. The RTP properties of CPDs are derived from the π → π* and n → π* electron transitions of those bonds. After that, the Si-O bonds on CPDs surface restricted the vibration and rotation of the phosphorescence sub-units, result in improvement in afterglow. This CPDs-6 owns long afterglow compared with previous reported RTP CDs without additional matrix [3,6,7,39–41]. Due to these excellent properties, CPDs-6 was meaningfully applied in information encryption and graphic anti-counterfeiting. The desired information can be easily encrypted and decrypted through UV irradiation. The graphic pattern is very clear. We also used silica layer cover the CPDs-6, thus maintaining phosphorescence characteristic in an air-saturated aqueous medium. The obtained CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution can produce RTP for about 10 s after the 365 nm lamp is extinguished. Since CPDs-6@SiO2 aqueous solution exhibits wonderful RTP, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, it was successfully used in biological imaging. Overall, CPDs-6 and CPDs-6@SiO2 have great potential in information encryption, graphic anti-counterfeiting and biological imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under grant no. 21774041, the National key research and development program of China (2016YFB0401701), and JLU Science and Technology Innovative Research Team 2017TD-06. Author Yang Liu and Author Weihua Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.11.120.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 1
